o 
i 
n 




Fair Political Practices Commission 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, Ca. 
95804 
Gentlemen; 
September 24, 1980 
I am City Attorney for the City of Portola, a general law City of 
approximately 2,000 people in Eastern Plumas County. 
We have been approached by a local real estate broker about the 
possibility of annexation of approximately 320 acres to the City 
in connection with a proposed subdivision therein. The proposal 
involves a projected mix of residential uses together with a golf 
course with related improvements. The projected 10-15 year huild-
out would (at 100% buildout) result in +/- 1,200 living units. 
The proposed area for annexation and subdivision is currently 
uninhabited territory owned by the Forest Service; it has been 
declared surplus and the prospective subdivider has lands suitable 
for a land exchange to enable him to acquire the proposed sub-
division site. 
Two members of the City Council own unimproved land which they 
independently acquired at least a year ago which abuts upon the 
land proposed as the area of annexation/subdivision. They own 
respectively a little more than one acre and a little more than 
three acres. The approximate value of the land is $10,000.00 an 
acre at current values. 
We would like to know if existing conflict law prohibits either 
or both councilmembers from voting on either annexation or the 
subdivision issues because of their land ownership. 
If more facts are needed, please call (916) 832-5845 or (916) 
832-4216 during ordinary business hours. 
Sincerely, 
Robert E. Roney 
City Attorney

sections.question: We would like to know if existing conflict law prohibits either or both councilmembers from voting on either annexation or the subdivision issues because of their land ownership.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether two city council members who own unimproved land adjacent to a proposed annexation and subdivision area are prohibited from voting on the annexation and subdivision matters under California conflict of interest law.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No CONCLUSION or ADVICE section present. Document is dated September 24, 1980 and provides detailed factual scenario. Contact information and requestor identification clearly stated.

embedding.summary: A city attorney for Portola, California requests FPPC guidance on whether two city council members who own adjacent land (approximately 1-3 acres valued at ~$10,000/acre) must recuse themselves from voting on a proposed 320-acre annexation and subdivision project involving a real estate broker's residential and golf course development proposal.
